# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Docker Compose configuration for a MongoDB development environment. Define MongoDB service with exposed ports for application connectivity. Set up volume mounts for data persistence. This is for our local development and testing setup.
# Category: mongodb_exposed_port

version: '3'  # specify version of docker compose file format (v2) in yml/toml as per the official Docker documentation here https://docs.docker.com/compose/overview/#specify-a-compose-file
services:   # define services for one or more containers to run your application inside a single server
  db:       # name of this service in docker-compose file is "db" (by default) and can be any alphanumeric string. This will serve as the identifier if you want, e.g., using `docker exec` command with database client like pymongo or MongoDB Compass
    image: mongo  # specify Docker Image to use for this service in docker-compose file (by default it uses official mongodb) from registry hub and tag as per requirement such version mentioned above. Here, we are using the latest stable build of ubuntu based images with all its dependencies installed along side MongoDB
    ports:  # expose defined port(s). Docker's standard exposed network stack is a UDP one (53) to which requests will be sent and another TCP on top for API calls. These can later also accessible through localhost interfaces, not just by IP address from where docker-machine ip command was run
      - "27017:27017"  # MongoDB's default port (for both http/https) to use and the exposed version of database service on host machine for this container. You can have more than one bind mount in a single volume, like two separate data volumes if you want persistent storage
    volumes:   # define directories or any content that should be available within Docker containers as files (bind-mounts) – each named file from here will get created on the host and then exposed to an active container. It'll allow persistence between restarts in case of failure, like a shared folder with data volume
      - dbdata:/var/lib/mongodb   # define where MongoDB’s databases should be stored using named volumes (in this example "db-volume") as Docker containers only have access to the content inside that directory. This makes it easy and quick setup for persistent storage by sharing data between running instances
  web:      # name of service in docker compose file, 'web'. It can also be any alphanumeric string but a common convention is using lowercase names like this if you want to have one-to-one correspondence with the Dockerfile or Compose files that define your multi container application.
    image: python  # specify Python as web service docker image in compose file by default, it's recommended when not specified otherwise on official images from registry hub such version mentioned above using tag like "python:3" (for versions higher than the latest stable build). In this case we are going with same for all services.
    ports:  # expose port(s) defined inside web service container in docker-compose file to be accessible outside of Dockerhost from localhost interface, can also use named network and IP or other containers using that name/ID as a bridge if not exposed anywhere else by default (uses standard TCP stack).
      - "5000:5000"  # define port inside web service container in docker-compose file to be accessible outside of Dockerhost from localhost interface, and can also use named network or IP other containers using that name/ID as a bridge if not exposed anywhere else by default (uses standard TCP stack).
    depends_on:   # specifies the dependencies on which services should start first. The service(s) defined after will be started before this one is done, for example when db and web are both up in normal operation mode or vice versa depending upon whether it’s true as mentioned here below otherwise Docker wouldn't run these two because they have circular dependencies
      - db   # if MongoDB container starts first then the following services would start only after DB is successful started. This means that by declaring web service dependency on database, we ensure our application to be properly initialized before it tries running due a lack of connection with mongoDb at this point which might occur while setting up and starting containers
    environment:  # Sets Environment variables for the Docker container(s) defined within `services` in compose file. This is how you set any configuration data or run command line parameters to pass when running your service from docker-compose CLI (or other tools like systemd, etc.) by default this will not be used until explicitly stated and documented as per the official documentation Docker docs here https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/service/.
      - MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME=root   # Sets environment variables to use when initializing a new database (default root user) and password must be defined as `MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD` in .env file or on docker-compose.yml if not using default env
      - MONGO_INITDB_DATABASE=mydb   # sets name of database that will initialize with mongo init (default is `mongodb`) and password must be defined as `MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD` in .env file or on docker-compose.yml if not using default env
      - MONGO_HOST=db   # Set the connection string for mongo, by convention we are setting it to point at db service and port (27017 is a common choice), but this can be anything you like depending on what your actual network setup requires. This will not have any value if docker compose file uses environment variable or command line argument
    links:  # link the name of container, when we want to access web service from db inside another host machine (default is same as linked services) but can be different based off conditions in `docker-compose.yml` and used for interconnected containers if required with specific settings or not depending on use cases
      - "db:27017"   # linking two instances of the db container inside current compose file (default uses exact name match) you can also set different conditions using links that have same format like `web:5432` if required and it will depend upon your specific use case.
  adminer:     # service to be used for tools testing in docker setup such as Adminer which is a MySQL Client GUI (MongoDB browser), you can specify image of this tool here using python version specified above like `adminerdb` or any other name by yourself if required and also set needed ports accordingly.
    image: adminer  # define Python Image for tools testing in docker compose file, it's recommended when not given otherwise the default from registry hub as mentioned before (python-based tag). And can be used with MongoDB browser `adminersdb` or any other name by yourself if required and also set needed ports accordingly.
    ports:  # expose port(s) defined inside Adminer service container in docker compose file to serve the tool for testing from localhost interface, same as web services but can be used named network IP address/name others containers using that bridge-if not exposed anywhere else by default (uses standard TCP stack).
      - "8080:8080"  # define port inside Adminer service container in docker compose file to serve the tool for testing from localhost interface, same as web services but can be used named network IP address/name others containers using that bridge-if not exposed anywhere else by default (uses standard TCP stack).
